TempoGap: An method to predicted the recovery status in patients

TeDing Chang, Hamed Moheimani, Das Jishnu, Timothy R. Billiar

2025-11-19

1. Introduction

TempoGap is an R package designed to quantify the temporal gap between the actual time since disease happened and the biological recovery time inferred from molecular or cellular data. This concept is important in whole period of disease, where biological recovery often lags behind clinical timelines. TempoGap provides a reproducible framework to:

  • estimate biological recovery predicted vs. actual recovery time.
  • compute the difference between predicted vs. actual recovery time
  • identify patients with delayed recovery trajectories.
  • support prognostic and monitoring applications in trauma or critical care patients.

This vignette introduces the scientific background, explains the method, and demonstrates typical workflows.

2. Scientific Background

Trauma induces strong immune activation followed by dysregulation. However, clinical time since injury does not always match molecular recovery. To address this, we model: - \(y\): actual time since injury. - \(x\): immune features (genes, proteins, etc.). - \(n\): number of samples - \(β\): coefficients describing contribution of each feature.

TempoGap estimates the immunological recovery time \(\hat{y}\) using the least absolute shrinkage and selection operator (LASSO) regression: \[ \hat{\beta}(\lambda) = \arg\min_{\beta} \left[ \frac{1}{2n} \lVert y - X\beta \rVert_{2}^{2} + \lambda \lVert \beta \rVert_{1} \right] \]

Then the temporal gap is calculated as: \[ \text{Gap} = \hat{y} - loess(y) \] There is the overview of TempoGap algorithm:

3. Installing TempoGap

# devtools::install_github("TempoGap")
library(TempoGap)
library(tidyverse)
## ── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
## ✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ readr     2.1.5
## ✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ stringr   1.5.2
## ✔ ggplot2   4.0.0     ✔ tibble    3.3.0
## ✔ lubridate 1.9.4     ✔ tidyr     1.3.1
## ✔ purrr     1.1.0     
## ── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
## ✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
## ✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
## ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors

4. Example Data

TempoGap can analyze:

  • bulk RNA-seq normalized counts
  • gene array data
  • other clinical indicators
  • cytokine profiles

As an example, we used the GlueGrant2 T-cell, PMN and Monocytes transcriptomic data to train the temporal gap model. The GlueGrant2 contains samples collected at multiple time points after injury, providing a suitable temporal structure for model development. The sampling distribution of the GlueGrant2 cohort includes the following time intervals since injury:

For this tutorial, we will use the T-cell subset from the GlueGrant2 dataset as an example. To begin, we load the T-cell data from GlueGrant2 into the R environment.

meta <- read.csv(file = "../Result/20251119_tcell_metadata.csv")
expr <- read.csv(file = "../Result/20251119_tcell_expr.csv",
                 check.names = F,
                 row.names = 1)

check the T cell object information:

str(meta)
## 'data.frame':    922 obs. of  32 variables:
##  $ Experiment.Names         : int  401283 401284 321460 321542 321543 321464 401339 401340 320348 320349 ...
##  $ Original.order           : int  182 330 2096 2237 2357 2462 1076 1622 2094 2353 ...
##  $ RECOVERY_CLASS           : chr  "Complicated" "Complicated" "Uncomplicated" "Uncomplicated" ...
##  $ MICROARRAY_ID            : int  18226751 29542962 31650682 21070665 22775117 13194569 13630755 19583298 5601004 21423372 ...
##  $ Extant.Microarray.DeID.  : chr  "yes" "yes" "yes" "yes" ...
##  $ PATIENT_ID               : chr  "10089333" "10089333" "10211506" "10211506" ...
##  $ HOURS_SINCE_INJURY       : num  6.8 26.6 7.3 29.9 103.1 ...
##  $ X0.1.4.7.14.21.28.days   : num  0.5 1 0.5 1 4 7 0.5 4 0.5 4 ...
##  $ ARRAY_SAMPLE_TYPE        : chr  "T-cell" "T-cell" "T-cell" "T-cell" ...
##  $ PMN.ARRAYS               : logi  NA NA NA NA NA NA ...
##  $ PMN.0.1.4.7.14.21.28.days: logi  NA NA NA NA NA NA ...
##  $ PMN.COMP.UNCOMP          : logi  NA NA NA NA NA NA ...
##  $ SAMPLE_STUDYSTART_DAYS   : int  0 1 0 1 4 7 0 1 0 1 ...
##  $ AGE                      : int  30 30 20 20 20 20 36 36 34 34 ...
##  $ SEX                      : chr  "M" "M" "M" "M" ...
##  $ MODEL_ID                 : chr  "trauma" "trauma" "trauma" "trauma" ...
##  $ PRIMARY_DEATH_CAUSE      : chr  NA NA NA NA ...
##  $ ICU_DAYS                 : int  42 42 4 4 4 4 13 13 3 3 ...
##  $ HOSPITAL_LENGTH_OF_STAY  : int  41 41 10 10 10 10 63 63 26 26 ...
##  $ SURVIVAL                 : chr  "Yes" "Yes" "Yes" "Yes" ...
##  $ HEIGHT                   : int  175 175 185 185 185 185 162 162 173 173 ...
##  $ WEIGHT                   : num  88 88 82.1 82.1 82.1 82.1 72 72 68.4 68.4 ...
##  $ FINAL_DISP               : chr  "07: Another acute care facility" "07: Another acute care facility" "06: Home" "06: Home" ...
##  $ NOSOCOMIAL_INFECTION     : chr  "01: Yes" "01: Yes" "02: No" "02: No" ...
##  $ SURGICAL_SITE_INFECTION  : chr  "01: Yes" "01: Yes" "02: No" "02: No" ...
##  $ VENT_ASSOCIATED_PNEUMONIA: chr  "01: Yes" "01: Yes" "02: No" "02: No" ...
##  $ TIME_TO_RECOVERY         : int  555 555 4 4 4 4 7 7 3 3 ...
##  $ MAX_MARSHALL_SCORE       : num  9.41 9.41 1.66 1.66 1.66 1.66 5.17 5.17 2.37 2.37 ...
##  $ MAX_DENVER_2_SCORE       : int  6 6 0 0 0 0 2 2 0 0 ...
##  $ PHASE_TRAUMA             : chr  "early" "mediate" "early" "mediate" ...
##  $ CLASS                    : chr  "Complicated_early" "Complicated_mediate" "Uncomplicated_early" "Uncomplicated_mediate" ...
##  $ DISEASE_STATUS           : chr  "trauma" "trauma" "trauma" "trauma" ...
dim(expr)
## [1] 14566   922

Check the data to make sure the colnames of expr is totally identical the experiment names of meta.

table(colnames(expr) == meta$Experiment.Names)
## 
## TRUE 
##  922

Step 1. extract the uncomplicated recovery patients as the baseline patients.

Subjects were stratified into three recovery trajectories: uncomplicated recovery (UCR; recovery < 5 days after injury), intermediate recovery (IR; 5 days ≤ recovery ≤ 14 days), and complicated recovery (CR; no recovery by day 28, recovery > 14 days, or death). Patients in the CR group were significantly older than those in the UCR group. Furthermore, the incidence of adverse outcomes, including ventilator-associated pneumonia and nosocomial infections, was markedly higher in the CR group. We will choose the uncomplicated recovery patients as the baseline of immune recovery model.

idx_ucr <- meta$RECOVERY_CLASS == "Uncomplicated"
table(idx_ucr)
## idx_ucr
## FALSE  TRUE 
##   690   232
expression_matrix <- as.matrix(expr[, idx_ucr])
time_vector <- meta$HOURS_SINCE_INJURY[idx_ucr]

Step 2. Training the immune recovery clock in uncomplicated recovery trauma patients.

After preparing all required data, we use the train_temporal_model function to train the immune recovery model for trauma patients. This function includes several parameters that can be adjusted based on the needs of your specific project.

  • cv_folds represents number of folds for cross-validation.

  • alpha represents elastic net mixing parameter. Default is 1 (LASSO). If alpha is a vector of continuous numbers, hyperparameter optimization of alpha will be performed based on the vector.

  • n_boot represents bootstrapping numbers.

Beyond trauma patients, the TempoGap framework can also be applied to other diseases that include longitudinal sampling. Any data set with multiple time points can be used to train disease-specific recovery trajectory models.

train_model <- train_temporal_model(expression_matrix = expression_matrix,
                                    time_vector = time_vector, 
                                    n_boot = 100,
                                    seed = 123)
##   alpha lambda_min  cvm_min

Step 3. Temporal gap

After obtained the normal recovery trajectory of trauma patients, we will use the predict_temporal_gap to obtain temporal gap between immune recovery time and the actual time point since injury. The parameter of predict_temporal_gap includes:

  • model: A trained cv.glmnet object return from train_temporal_model function.

  • expression_matrix: A numeric matrix (features x samples) of gene/protein/lipid… expression values for prediction.

  • actual_time: A numeric vector of actual post-injury hours (for computing TempoGap).

  • n_boot: Bootstrapping numbers used in train_temporal_gap function

# We need to remove the healthy contorls.
idx_control <- meta$RECOVERY_CLASS == "Control"
x_test <- expr[,!idx_control]
y_test <- meta$HOURS_SINCE_INJURY[!idx_control]
# temporal gap
predicted_results <- predict_temporal_gap(model = train_model$model,
                                          expression_matrix = as.matrix(x_test),
                                          actual_time = y_test,
                                          n_boot = 100)

Step 4. Visualization

We can use the plot_bootstrap_coefficients function to visualize the coefficient stability and contributions within our model:

plot_bootstrap_coefficients(coef_matrix = train_model$coef_matrix,
                            top_n = 50)

We can use the plot_temporal_gap_distribution function to visualize the correlation between predicted time and actual time:

plot_temporal_gap_distribution(gap_data = predicted_results$predicted_temporal_gap)
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Step 5. Cox hazard regression

If you would like to examine the association between the temporal gap and clinical indicators—such as recovery time or ICU discharge days. You can use the cox_analysis_tempo_gap function.

event <- ifelse(meta$TIME_TO_RECOVERY > 30, 0, 1)[!idx_control]
time_to_event <- meta$TIME_TO_RECOVERY[!idx_control]
cox_results <- cox_analysis_tempo_gap(tempo_gap = predicted_results$predicted_temporal_gap$ScaledTempoGap,
                                      time_to_event = time_to_event,
                                      event = event,
                                      cut_method = "none")
## Loading required package: survival
## Loading required package: survminer
## Loading required package: ggpubr
## 
## Attaching package: 'survminer'
## The following object is masked from 'package:survival':
## 
##     myeloma
cox_results$summary
## Call:
## coxph(formula = cox_formula, data = df)
## 
##   n= 900, number of events= 815 
##    (2 observations deleted due to missingness)
## 
##              coef exp(coef) se(coef)     z Pr(>|z|)    
## tempo_gap 0.24265   1.27462  0.03221 7.533 4.97e-14 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
##           exp(coef) exp(-coef) lower .95 upper .95
## tempo_gap     1.275     0.7845     1.197     1.358
## 
## Concordance= 0.588  (se = 0.011 )
## Likelihood ratio test= 55.96  on 1 df,   p=7e-14
## Wald test            = 56.74  on 1 df,   p=5e-14
## Score (logrank) test = 56.44  on 1 df,   p=6e-14
